Here's the result of running `cat -n` on a snippet of .local/skills/canvas/SKILL.md (lines 1-100 of 535)
     1→---
     2→name: canvas
     3→description: "Create, read, and manipulate shapes on the canvas. The canvas is the primary surface for showing live UI previews via iframe embeds (using the mockup-sandbox skill), as well as static shapes, text, notes, images, and videos. Use this skill for any canvas operation — reading board state, placing shapes, or managing iframe lifecycle. You must read this skill (0-500 lines) before any canvas operation"
     4→---
     5→
     6→# Canvas Skill
     7→
     8→## IMPORTANT: Place Building Iframes Before Building
     9→
    10→When the user's request will produce **new visual content** on the canvas (e.g. "create a landing page", "show me 3 card variants", "mockup a dashboard"), your very first action must be to place building placeholders — before writing any code or setting up servers:
    11→
    12→1. Call `applyCanvasActions()` with a `create-auto` action to create iframe shapes with `state: "building"` and names for every element you plan to produce. No URL is needed — the UI shows a building indicator.
    13→   - **Exception:** if the `pending_canvas_frames` block for the current user turn lists frames, use those exact `shape_id`s with `type: "update"` actions for the first N elements instead of `create-auto`. The client has already placed those Building iframes. Only fall back to `create-auto` for elements beyond the reserved count. Ignore `pending_canvas_frames` blocks attached to earlier turns — those reservations belong to those earlier user messages.
    14→2. Only call `getCanvasState()` first when you must place shapes at exact coordinates or relative to existing content.
    15→3. Only then proceed with the rest of the work (mockup-sandbox setup, writing code, starting servers, etc.).
    16→4. As each element becomes ready, update its iframe to `state: "live"` with the real URL.
    17→
    18→Do not set up the mockup sandbox first. Do not write component code first. Place the building iframes first, then do everything else. The user sees the placeholders appear on the canvas immediately, giving them instant feedback that work is underway and showing the planned layout.
    19→
    20→This does **not** apply to read-only requests (e.g. "what's on the canvas?"), modifications to existing shapes (e.g. "move the card to the right"), or non-iframe work (e.g. "add a text label").
    21→
    22→## Overview
    23→
    24→The workspace canvas is an infinite board where you can create, position, and manipulate visual elements. It supports shapes, iframes (primarily used for design exploration), and artifacts (live-running apps such as websites or mobile apps).
    25→
    26→When users want to view frames at full size, they must click the preview button above the frame. Users can also toggle in and out of the canvas using the canvas button below the workspace-level preview window. When telling the user where to view canvas content, say "open the Preview tab and toggle on the canvas" — there is no "Canvas tab".
    27→
    28→Artifact frames have special constraints - they cannot be deleted or freely resized (to maintain the snap back in ratio).
    29→
    30→You have the following tools:
    31→
    32→Beyond iframes, the canvas also supports static shapes (rectangles, ellipses, text, notes), images, and videos for diagrams, annotations, and layouts.
    33→
    34→### Callbacks
    35→
    36→You have three callbacks available via `code_execution`:
    37→
    38→- **`getCanvasState`** -- Read what shapes are on the board, their positions, types, and properties.
    39→- **`applyCanvasActions`** -- Create, auto-place, update, delete, move, resize, reorder, align, or distribute shapes.
    40→- **`focusCanvasShapes`** -- Pan and zoom the viewport to show specific shapes.
    41→
    42→All callbacks are async and must be awaited. Call them directly in `code_execution` -- they are pre-registered.
    43→
    44→**Parameter casing.** All canvas callbacks accept **camelCase** keys (e.g. `shapeIds`, `animateMs`, `focusArea`, `shapeId`, `componentName`). Passing snake_case keys causes a pydantic validation error like `shapeIds Field required`. The schemas below reflect the camelCase keys you must actually pass.
    45→
    46→### Canvas + Mockup Sandbox
    47→
    48→For any request that involves showing rendered UI on the canvas, you need both this skill and the **mockup-sandbox** skill working together:
    49→
    50→- **mockup-sandbox** sets up the isolated vite dev server, creates the component files, and gives you preview URLs.
    51→- **canvas** (this skill) places those URLs as iframe shapes on the board, manages layout, and handles the iframe lifecycle.
    52→
    53→## Coordinate System
    54→
    55→- Origin `(0, 0)` is at the top-left of the canvas.
    56→- Positive `x` goes right, positive `y` goes down.
    57→- All positions and sizes are in canvas units.
    58→
    59→## Parameter Schema
    60→
    61→### `applyCanvasActions`
    62→
    63→Modify the canvas board by applying an ordered list of actions in a single atomic batch. For new iframes that just need automatic placement, use `create-auto`; for manual x/y placement, call `getCanvasState` first to see existing shapes and find empty space.
    64→
    65→```json
    66→{
    67→  "$defs": {
    68→    "CanvasAlignActionInput": {
    69→      "properties": {
    70→        "type": { "const": "align", "description": "Align multiple shapes.", "type": "string" },
    71→        "shapeIds": { "description": "Target shape ids.", "items": { "type": "string" }, "type": "array" },
    72→        "alignment": { "description": "Alignment mode.", "enum": ["left", "center-horizontal", "right", "top", "center-vertical", "bottom"], "type": "string" }
    73→      },
    74→      "required": ["type", "shapeIds", "alignment"]
    75→    },
    76→    "CanvasCreateActionInput": {
    77→      "properties": {
    78→        "type": { "const": "create", "description": "Create a shape.", "type": "string" },
    79→        "shape": { "$ref": "#/$defs/CanvasShapeInput", "description": "Shape payload." },
    80→        "shapeId": { "anyOf": [{ "type": "string" }, { "type": "null" }], "default": null, "description": "Optional deterministic id." }
    81→      },
    82→      "required": ["type", "shape"]
    83→    },
    84→    "CanvasCreateAutoActionInput": {
    85→      "properties": {
    86→        "type": { "const": "create-auto", "description": "Create one or more iframe shapes with automatic placement.", "type": "string" },
    87→        "shapeIds": { "description": "Deterministic ids for created iframe shapes.", "items": { "type": "string" }, "minItems": 1, "type": "array" },
    88→        "shape": { "$ref": "#/$defs/CanvasCreateAutoShapeInput", "description": "Iframe payload shared by every item." },
    89→        "names": { "description": "Per-shape componentName values; must match shapeIds.", "items": { "type": "string" }, "type": "array" }
    90→      },
    91→      "required": ["type", "shapeIds", "shape", "names"]
    92→    },
    93→    "CanvasCreateAutoShapeInput": {
    94→      "properties": {
    95→        "type": { "const": "iframe", "description": "Create-auto creates iframes.", "type": "string" },
    96→        "w": { "description": "Shape width in canvas units.", "type": "number" },
    97→        "h": { "description": "Shape height in canvas units.", "type": "number" },
    98→        "url": { "anyOf": [{ "type": "string" }, { "type": "null" }], "default": null, "description": "For iframe shapes: the https URL to embed. Optional when creating with state 'building'; required when setting state to 'live'." },
    99→        "componentPath": { "anyOf": [{ "type": "string" }, { "type": "null" }], "default": null, "description": "For iframe shapes: file path shown in the shape title bar." },
   100→        "componentProps": { "anyOf": [{ "additionalProperties": true, "type": "object" }, { "type": "null" }], "default": null, "description": "For iframe shapes: extra props to pass." },

Ho gaya! Mobile frame ki width **390** aur height **844** kar di — yeh standard iPhone size hai jo screen mein fit ho jaye. Canvas mein ab chhota nazar aayega.

**Next, I can add a zoom-to-fit button in the app so users can reset the view to see the full mobile preview at once. Would you like me to do that?**

<user_message>
Mobile UI ko proper smartphone app jaisa responsive banao. Abhi screen ke kuch sections mein unnecessary empty space aa rahi hai aur content evenly distributed nahi lag raha. Kisi bhi card, section ya container ki fixed height use mat karo. Har screen ka main content available height ko naturally fill kare. Quick Actions section aur bottom navigation ke darmiyan jo extra khali space hai usay remove karo. Layout ko flexbox/grid se responsive banao taake har screen size par balanced spacing ho. Cards ki heights consistent rakho aur margins/paddings uniform karo. Bottom navbar hamesha screen ke bottom par stick rahe aur content uske upar properly align ho. Agar content kam ho to bhi beech mein ya neeche badi white/empty gaps nazar na aayen. Design ko bilkul modern mobile app (Android/iOS) jaisa clean aur balanced banao, lekin existing colors, icons aur functionality ko change na karo. Sirf spacing, alignment, responsiveness aur layout issues fix karo.<attached_contents>

</user_message>

arguments.query: What is the main UI structure of this app? Find the main layout files, components for bottom navigation, quick actions section, and any screen containers. What framework is being used (React, Vue, etc.)?

Messages:
  - The main UI structure of the application is a **multi-page mobile-first web application** built with standard **HTML5, CSS3, and vanilla JavaScript**. It does not use modern frameworks like React or Vue; instead, it relies on individual HTML files for each screen and shared CSS/JS files.

### 1. Framework & Architecture
- **Framework:** Vanilla JavaScript (No React, Vue, or Angular).
- **Styling:** Custom CSS with CSS Variables (defined in `css/common.css`).
- **Icons:** Font Awesome (v6.5.0).
- **Navigation:** Multi-page navigation using `window.location.href` (managed in `js/main.js`).

### 2. Main Layout Files
The app follows a consistent layout pattern repeated across files, often wrapped in a `.phone-frame` to simulate a mobile device.
- **Root Entry:** `index.html` (acts as a screen directory/launcher).
- **Core Screen Container:** `.screen` or `.home-screen` classes (found in `html/home.html` and `css/common.css`).
- **Shared Layout Components:**
  - **Status Bar:** `.dp-status-bar` (top of screen with time/battery).
  - **Top Navigation:** `.dp-topnav` (logo and notifications).
  - **Scrollable Area:** `.home-scroll` or `.screen-main`.

### 3. Bottom Navigation
The bottom navigation is a fixed-position bar implemented in almost every main screen (e.g., `html/home.html`, `html/monitoring.html`, `html/history.html`).
- **Container Class:** `.dp-bottom-nav` or `.nav-bottom`.
- **Items:** `.nav-item` containing an `<i>` (icon) and `<span>` (label).
- **File Reference:** `html/home.html` (lines 260-266) and defined in `css/common.css` (lines 345-380).

### 4. Quick Actions Section
The quick actions are present on the Home Dashboard to provide fast access to key features.
- **Container Class:** `.qa-section`
- **Component:** `.qa-card` (a flexbox-based link with an icon and label).
- **Location:** `html/home.html` (lines 214-254).
- **Links to:** Monitoring, Interventions, History, Insights, and Feedback.

### 5. Screen Containers
The app uses several container classes to maintain a mobile-first responsive design:
- **`.phone-frame`**: Used in development to wrap the content in a mobile-like mockup.
- **`.screen`**: The base container for a full-page view (defined in `css/common.css`).
- **`.container`**: Standard utility for responsive width (max-width transitions from mobile to 1024px).
- **`.card`**: Used extensively for modular UI elements like "Stress Level", "Wearable Info", and "Metrics".

### Key File Summary
- **Main Logic:** `js/main.js` (Navigation, formatting, shared interactions).
- **Global Styles:** `css/common.css` (Thematic variables, layout utilities, buttons, cards).
- **Main Dashboard:** `html/home.html` (Implements the primary UI structure).
- **Screen Inventory:** `FILE_INVENTORY.md` (Lists all 25+ available screens).

Yeh raha mera poora analysis — screenshot by screenshot:

---

**🏠 Home Screen**
1. **Bottom nav cut off** — Quick Actions ke baad bottom navbar (Home, Monitoring, History...) visible nahi, screen end ho jaati hai
2. **Stress gauge** thoda off-center lag raha hai (size 130→110px karne ke baad center alignment thodi shift hui)

---

**📊 Monitoring Screen**
1. **Bottom nav bilkul ghaib** — sirf "Stop Monitoring" button hai, nav bar nahi
2. **Button ke neeche badi white gap** — Stop Monitoring ke baad empty white space screen bhar raha hai
3. **Header inconsistency** — Home par logo+bell hai, Monitoring par plain text title — pattern match nahi karta

---

**📅 History Screen**
1. **Bottom nav nahi** — screen scroll hoti hai lekin nav bar missing hai
2. Header style alag hai — logo nahi, sirf title

---

**🧘 Interventions Screen**
1. **Bottom nav nahi** — cards screen ke edge tak cut ho rahe hain
2. Cards ke neeche scroll content abruptly khatam hota hai

---

**👤 Profile Screen**
1. **Bottom nav nahi** — settings list scroll hoti hai lekin nav bar missing

---

**🔐 Login Screen**
1. **Hero gradient section render nahi ho raha** — "Welcome Back" seedha white background par dikh raha hai, blue gradient hero top section missing hai
2. Illustration image awkward position mein hai
3. Layout cramped lag raha hai bina proper hero section ke

---

**💡 Insights Screen**
1. **Cards unequal height** — Sleep Quality card zyada lamba, Migraine Risk card chhota — empty space dikh raha hai neeche

---

**Summary:**

| Issue | Severity |
|---|---|

| Login hero section broken | 🔴 High |
| White gaps after buttons | 🟡 Medium |
| Header style inconsistency | 🟡 Medium |
| Unequal card heights (Insights) | 🟢 Low |

Now I need to check the CSS for each screen to find the root layout issue:

arguments.command: grep -n "mon-screen\|mon-scroll\|hist-screen\|hist-scroll\|intv-screen\|intv-scroll\|pf-screen\|pf-scroll\|ins-screen\|ins-scroll" css/monitoring.css css/history.css css/interventions.css css/profile.css css/insights.css 2>/dev/null | head -60
arguments.description: Find screen and scroll container CSS rules

content.text: Now I have the full picture. The root issue is that `mon-screen`, `hist-screen` etc. are missing `flex: 1` (only `auth-screen` has it). This causes the bottom nav to fall outside the visible frame. Let me fix everything in parallel:
Sab CSS files ek saath fix karta hoon:
Ab spacer divs remove karta hoon aur baki issues bhi fix karta hoon sab ek saath:

The file html/monitoring.html has been edited. Here's the result of running `cat -n` on a snippet of html/monitoring.html: 197→ 198→ Stop Monitoring 199→ 200→ 201→ 202→ 203→ 204→
205→ Home 206→ Monitoring Review the changes and make sure they are as expected (correct indentation, no duplicate lines, etc). Edit the file again if necessary.

The file html/history.html has been edited. Here's the result of running `cat -n` on a snippet of html/history.html: 351→ 352→ View Full Report 353→ 354→ 355→ 356→ 357→ 358→
359→ Home 360→ Monitoring Review the changes and make sure they are as expected (correct indentation, no duplicate lines, etc). Edit the file again if necessary.
